name: Web Performance
description: Understand Core Web Vitals, rendering pipeline, caching strategies, and optimization techniques that separate seniors from juniors.

cards.back:
Google's key metrics for user experience:

• LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): <2.5s
  How fast the main content loads. Measured by the largest image or text block in the viewport.

• INP (Interaction to Next Paint): <200ms
  How fast the page responds to user input. Replaced FID in 2024.

• CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): <0.1
  How much the page layout shifts unexpectedly. Caused by images without dimensions, injected content, web fonts.

These directly impact SEO rankings and user retention.

cards.front: What is the Critical Rendering Path?
cards.back:
The sequence of steps the browser takes to render a page:

1. Parse HTML → DOM tree
2. Parse CSS → CSSOM tree
3. Combine DOM + CSSOM → Render tree
4. Layout — calculate geometry (position, size)
5. Paint — fill in pixels (colors, borders, shadows)
6. Composite — combine layers and display

Optimizations:
• Minimize render-blocking CSS/JS
• Inline critical CSS
• Defer non-critical JS (defer/async attributes)
• Reduce DOM depth and complexity
• Use will-change for composite-only animations

cards.front: What is code splitting and how does it improve performance?
cards.back:
Code splitting breaks your bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand.

Route-based splitting:
const Settings = lazy(() => import('./Settings'));

Component-based splitting:
const HeavyChart = lazy(() => import('./Chart'));

How it helps:
• Smaller initial bundle → faster first load
• Users only download code they need
• Better caching (unchanged chunks stay cached)

Tools: React.lazy(), dynamic import(), webpack/Vite chunk splitting, Next.js automatic route splitting.

Rule of thumb: Split at route boundaries first, then heavy components.

Tree shaking eliminates unused code from your final bundle.

How it works:
• Bundler analyzes ES module import/export statements
• Unused exports are removed from the bundle
• Only works with static ES modules (import/export), NOT CommonJS (require)

Example:
// math.js exports add, subtract, multiply
import { add } from './math';
// subtract and multiply are tree-shaken out

To ensure tree shaking works:
• Use ES modules
• Avoid side effects in modules
• Set "sideEffects": false in package.json
• Avoid barrel files that re-export everything

cards.front: What are the different image optimization strategies?
cards.back:
1. Format: Use modern formats
   • WebP (30% smaller than JPEG)
   • AVIF (50% smaller than JPEG)
   • Use <picture> for format fallbacks

2. Sizing:
   • Serve different sizes: srcset + sizes attributes
   • Never serve larger images than displayed

3. Loading:
   • loading="lazy" for below-fold images
   • fetchpriority="high" for hero images
   • Decode asynchronously: decoding="async"

4. Always set width/height to prevent CLS

5. Use CDN with automatic optimization (Cloudflare Images, Next.js Image, Imgix)

cards.front: What is the difference between defer and async for scripts?
cards.back:
<script> — blocks HTML parsing, downloads and executes immediately

<script async> — downloads in parallel with HTML parsing, executes immediately when ready (order not guaranteed)

<script defer> — downloads in parallel, executes AFTER HTML parsing, in order

When to use:
• defer: Most scripts (maintains order, doesn't block)
• async: Independent scripts (analytics, ads) that don't depend on DOM or other scripts
• Neither: Scripts that must run before page renders (rare)

<script type="module"> is deferred by default.

cards.front: What caching strategies should you know?
cards.back:
Browser caching:
• Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable (for hashed assets)
• Cache-Control: no-cache (always revalidate with server)
• ETag / Last-Modified for conditional requests

Service Worker caching:
• Cache First: serve from cache, update in background
• Network First: try network, fall back to cache
• Stale While Revalidate: serve stale, update cache

Application caching:
• React Query / SWR for API response caching
• useMemo / React.memo for computed value caching
• CDN edge caching for static assets

Key: Hash filenames (app.abc123.js) + long cache = perfect caching.

cards.front: What causes layout thrashing and how do you avoid it?

cards.back:
Rendering only the visible items in a long list instead of all items at once.

Without virtualization:
10,000 items = 10,000 DOM nodes = slow, high memory

With virtualization:
10,000 items but only ~20 DOM nodes (what's visible + buffer)

How it works:
1. Calculate which items are visible based on scroll position
2. Only render those items
3. Use absolute positioning or transforms to place them correctly
4. Recycle DOM nodes as user scrolls

Libraries: @tanstack/virtual, react-window, react-virtuoso

Use when: Lists > 100 items, complex row components, or measured performance issues.

PRPL is a pattern for structuring and serving web apps for maximum performance:

P — Push critical resources (preload, HTTP/2 push)
R — Render initial route as fast as possible
P — Pre-cache remaining routes (service worker)
L — Lazy-load other routes on demand

Implementation:
• Inline critical CSS in <head>
• Preload key resources: <link rel="preload">
• Service worker to cache app shell
• Code split routes with dynamic import()
• Prefetch likely next routes: <link rel="prefetch">

This achieves instant subsequent navigations after the first load.

cards.front: How do you measure frontend performance?
cards.back:
Lab tools (dev environment):
• Lighthouse (Chrome DevTools) — overall audit
• Chrome Performance tab — flame charts, bottlenecks
• WebPageTest — multi-location testing
• Bundle analyzer (webpack-bundle-analyzer)

Field tools (real users):
• Chrome User Experience Report (CrUX)
• web-vitals library: import { onLCP, onINP, onCLS } from 'web-vitals'
• Performance Observer API
• Real User Monitoring (RUM) services

Key API:
performance.mark('start');
// ... work ...
performance.mark('end');
performance.measure('task', 'start', 'end');

Always measure in the field — lab results can differ significantly.

cards.front: What are Web Workers and when should you use them?
What is resource hints and preloading?
cards.back: Resource hints tell the browser about resources you'll need: — Download NOW, needed for current page — Download when idle, needed for NEXT navigation — Establish connection early (DNS + TCP + TLS) — DNS lookup only (cheaper than preconnect) fetchpriority="high" on or

cards.front: What is the difference between SSR, SSG, and CSR?
cards.back:
CSR (Client-Side Rendering):
• Browser downloads empty HTML + JS bundle
• JS renders the page
• Slow first paint, fast subsequent navigation

SSR (Server-Side Rendering):
• Server renders HTML for each request
• Browser shows content immediately, then hydrates
• Fast first paint, more server load

SSG (Static Site Generation):
• HTML generated at BUILD time
• Served from CDN, fastest possible
• Content only updates on rebuild

ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration):
• SSG + background revalidation
• Best of both: CDN speed + fresh content

Choose based on: How dynamic is the data? How important is SEO?

cards.front: What causes Cumulative Layout Shift and how do you fix it?
cards.back:
CLS measures unexpected visual shifts. Common causes:

1. Images without dimensions
   Fix: Always set width and height, or use aspect-ratio

2. Ads/embeds without reserved space
   Fix: Use min-height or aspect-ratio containers

3. Web fonts causing FOUT/FOIT
   Fix: font-display: swap + preload fonts
   Better: size-adjust or font-metric-override

4. Dynamically injected content
   Fix: Reserve space with placeholders/skeletons

5. Late-loading CSS
   Fix: Inline critical CSS, preload stylesheets

Measure with: Layout Instability API or web-vitals library
